answer:
**Iqbal's "Sayed ki Loh-e-Turbat" (Bang-E-Dara Part 1, ch. 38) and the Old English "The Wanderer" (Old English Elegies, ch. 11) both elegize lost leaders—the former an epitaph-like inscription for Sir Syed Ahmed Khan (a 19th-century Muslim reformer), the latter a thane's lament for his unnamed "gold-friend" (gold-lord).**[^1][^2] **Yet they differ markedly in voice, focus, cultural worldview, and resolution.**

**Voice and perspective set them apart most sharply. "The Wanderer" is a first-person monologue from the exiled survivor, consumed by personal grief over his lord's death, kinsmen's slaughter, and ensuing isolation: "Often I must alone... lament my sorrows. There is none now living to whom I dare my heart's secrets openly speak."**[^3] **The speaker wanders frost-bound seas, haunted by memories of mead-hall joys and his "gold-friend" who "the earth's darkness covered."**[^4] **Iqbal's poem, by contrast, personifies the tombstone itself ("my very tombstone is enraptured by my speech"), speaking *from* the grave in a prophetic, second-person address to the living reader ("O you, whose bird of soul is by the thread of breath confined").**[^5][^6] **Sir Syed's "spirit's bird" is liberated, surveying his life's "harvest," not lamenting loss.**

**Purpose diverges too: "The Wanderer" broods on *transience* (wyrd/fate), ruins, and *ubi sunt* motifs ("Where has the horse gone? Where has the warrior?"), evolving toward stoic wisdom and heavenly consolation.**[^7] **Iqbal's inscription celebrates achievement ("This is the gathering that was my constant, anxious thought") while exhorting reform: teach unity, shun division, wield "poetry's great might" against "falsehood."**[^8][^9] **Anglo-Saxon fatalism yields to Iqbal's activist Islamic optimism.**

**Worldviews reflect eras: the Wanderer invokes a decaying "middle-earth," storm-battered halls, wolves devouring the dead—pagan-heroic echoes fused with Christian patience ("Good is he who... seeks grace... from the Father in heaven").**[^10] **Iqbal urges modern statesmanship ("Courage is the staff of those who master statecraft's design"), pen as "Jamshid's Cup," and anti-hypocrisy in a colonial Muslim context.**[^11]

**Formally, "The Wanderer" (prose layout in translation, ~882 words) builds through dream-kisses of the lost lord, awakening to "fallow waves," ending in secret counsel.**[^12] **Iqbal's verse (15 stanzas, 320 words) mimics an animated inscription, imperative couplets culminating in fiery call: "set the harvest of all falsehood alight!"**[^13]

**In sum, "The Wanderer" internalizes loss for solitary wisdom amid inevitable decay; Iqbal's animates the leader's legacy to ignite communal revival.**

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 38 ("Sayed Ki Loh-e-Turbat"), [translation](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/38-sayed-ki-loh-e-turbat/translation/).
[^2]: Unknown, *Old English Elegies*, ch. 11 ("The Wanderer"), [translation](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/11-the-wanderer/translation/).
[^3]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 11, [p-9462cc](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/11-the-wanderer/translation/#p-9462cc).
[^4]: Ibid., [p-9462cc](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/11-the-wanderer/translation/#p-9462cc).
[^5]: *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 38, [p-f55cb3](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/38-sayed-ki-loh-e-turbat/translation/#p-f55cb3).
[^6]: Ibid., [p-3ab597](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/38-sayed-ki-loh-e-turbat/translation/#p-3ab597).
[^7]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 11, [p-9cca5e](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/11-the-wanderer/translation/#p-9cca5e) to [p-5bdc65](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/11-the-wanderer/translation/#p-5bdc65).
[^8]: *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 38, [p-6189a3](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/38-sayed-ki-loh-e-turbat/translation/#p-6189a3).
[^9]: Ibid., [p-2e3845](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/38-sayed-ki-loh-e-turbat/translation/#p-2e3845).
[^10]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 11, [p-5bdc65](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/11-the-wanderer/translation/#p-5bdc65).
[^11]: *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 38, [p-ee0ccd](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/38-sayed-ki-loh-e-turbat/translation/#p-ee0ccd) and [p-77c740](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/38-sayed-ki-loh-e-turbat/translation/#p-77c740).
[^12]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 11, full [translation](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/11-the-wanderer/translation/).
[^13]: *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 38, full [translation](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/38-sayed-ki-loh-e-turbat/translation/).
